passages.explanation: On a Sabbath Jesus goes to eat in the house of a prominent Pharisee, and he's being carefully watched. A man suffering from abnormal swelling of his body is there in front of him. Jesus asks the Pharisees and experts in the law: is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not? They remain silent. He takes hold of the man, heals him, and sends him on his way. Then he asks: if one of you has a child or an ox that falls into a well on the Sabbath day, won't you immediately pull it out? They have nothing to say. Jesus keeps forcing the same question: is the Sabbath for restricting compassion or expressing it? Their silence is the answer they won't give out loud.

When Jesus notices how the guests pick the places of honour at the table, he tells them a parable: when someone invites you to a wedding feast, don't take the place of honour — someone more distinguished may have been invited. Then the host would tell you to give up your seat and you'd be humiliated, taking the lowest place. Instead, take the lowest place, so when the host comes he may say: friend, move up to a better place. Then you'll be honoured before everyone. For all who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted. Then he tells the host: when you give a luncheon or dinner, don't invite your friends, brothers, relatives, or rich neighbours — they may invite you back and you'll be repaid. Instead, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind. You will be blessed — they can't repay you, and you'll be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous. Jesus redefines social strategy: generosity that can't be reciprocated is the kind God rewards.

Someone at the table says: blessed is the one who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God! Jesus tells a parable: a man prepares a great banquet and invites many guests. At the time of the banquet he sends his servant to tell those invited: come, everything is ready. But they all begin to make excuses. The first says he's bought a field and must go see it. The second has bought five yoke of oxen and is on his way to try them out. The third says he's just married. The servant reports back. The master is angry and tells the servant: go quickly into the streets and alleys and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame. After this is done, there's still room. The master says: go to the roads and country lanes and compel them to come in, so my house will be full. I tell you, not one of those who were invited will get a taste of my banquet. The excuses are all about property and comfort — legitimate things that have become obstacles. The kingdom feast goes to those who have nothing better to do.

Large crowds are travelling with Jesus. He turns and says: if anyone comes to me and doesn't hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters — yes, even their own life — such a person can't be my disciple. Whoever doesn't carry their cross and follow me can't be my disciple. Which of you, wanting to build a tower, doesn't first sit down and estimate the cost? Otherwise, you lay the foundation and can't finish, and everyone who sees it ridicules you. Or what king, going to war against another king, doesn't first sit down and consider whether he can oppose the one coming against him with twenty thousand? If not, he sends a delegation while the other is still far away and asks for terms of peace. In the same way, anyone who doesn't give up everything they have can't be my disciple. Salt is good, but if it loses its saltiness, how can it be made salty again? It's fit neither for the soil nor the manure pile — it's thrown out. Jesus doesn't soften the cost to attract a bigger crowd — he increases it. The word 'hate' is Semitic hyperbole for priority: loyalty to Jesus must be so total that every other allegiance looks like hatred by comparison. Count the cost before you commit.
